H-OS-L1.name: Pebble
H-OS-L1.sphere: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80) is the highest-n protocol in the HEALING series and sits at strong confidence. The distribution is broadly unimodal with a mode at 6 (19 responses), a right shoulder extending to 9, and minor left-tail mass at 3–4. The mean is modestly below the Anxiety subcategory, consistent with the different regulatory target. No bimodal structure detected; the spread reflects expected population diversity.
H-OS-L1.sphere_plain: Pebble has the most responses of any protocol in the collection, which gives us good confidence in the picture it paints. Most listeners report meaningful benefit, with results concentrated around the 6–8 range. A small number find it less helpful — likely people whose sensory experience of the music doesn't connect. Broadly reliable as an entry point for over-stimulation.

H-OS-L2.name: Rainfall
H-OS-L2.sphere: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85) shows a modest improvement over L1 in mean with comparable std, suggesting the protocol arc is functioning as designed. The distribution has a dual peak at 7 and 8 (15 responses combined) with a secondary mass at 4. The std remains consistent with population diversity rather than bimodality; the left-tail mass at 4 (7 responses) may reflect a subpopulation with limited responsiveness to longer DESCENT formats.
H-OS-L2.sphere_plain: Rainfall shows a modest improvement over Pebble, with more responses in the higher range and a pattern that suggests the arc is working as intended. A small cluster of listeners appears to respond less well — possibly those who find longer protocols harder to engage with when overstimulated. Worth keeping an eye on as more data comes in.

H-OS-L3.name: Glacier
H-OS-L3.sphere: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78) reaches meaningful confidence with the highest mean in the Over-stimulation subcategory. The distribution is relatively symmetric around 7–8, with tails present but not heavy. The ascending mean across the OS arc (6.55 → 6.71 → 6.98) is a clean and encouraging signal, consistent with the phi-sigmoid architecture producing stronger entrainment at longer durations for this listener group.
H-OS-L3.sphere_plain: Glacier is the strongest performer in the Over-stimulation group, with the highest average score of the three and a fairly balanced spread. The pattern across all three levels — gently improving as you go deeper — is encouraging. Longer protocols appear to be doing more for this listener group, which aligns with how the music is designed to work.

H-AX-L1.name: Meadow
H-AX-L1.sphere: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89) is the highest-mean protocol in the HEALING series and sits at strong confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with modal response at 6 and 8-9, and a thin low tail, suggesting broad positive response with moderate spread. The std of 1.89 is among the higher values in the Anxiety subcategory and warrants monitoring for a latent bimodal structure as n grows, though current shape does not confirm one.
H-AX-L1.sphere_plain: Meadow is one of the better-performing protocols in the collection, with most listeners reporting meaningful relief from anxiety and stress. Results do vary from person to person — some find it very helpful, a small number less so — but overall the signal is encouraging and based on enough responses to feel reliable.

H-AX-L2.name: Cottage
H-AX-L2.sphere: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.9, std=1.63) reaches strong confidence with a tighter distribution than L1. The modal cluster sits at 7 (16 responses) with a clean right-skew and minimal low-end mass. The reduced std relative to L1 may reflect self-selection: listeners who progress to L2 may have greater prior familiarity with the format, producing more homogeneous outcomes.
H-AX-L2.sphere_plain: Cottage shows a tighter, more consistent pattern than Meadow, with most responses clustering around the higher end of the scale. Listeners who arrive at L2 seem to respond a bit more predictably — which likely reflects that they already have some experience with the format. A solid, steady performer.

H-AX-L3.name: Valley
H-AX-L3.sphere: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81) sits at meaningful confidence with a distribution that broadly mirrors L1 in shape, including a notable tail above 8. The consistency of mean scores across the AX arc (7.14 → 6.90 → 6.97) is a positive signal; there is no evidence of quality degradation at deeper levels. The std remains moderate; subgroup investigation not yet indicated.
H-AX-L3.sphere_plain: Valley holds up well across a meaningful number of responses, with scores staying consistent with the earlier levels in the Anxiety series. There's no sign of the experience becoming less effective at the deeper level — if anything, it holds its own. A positive result for the arc as a whole.

H-CR-L1.name: Anchor
H-CR-L1.sphere: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71) is the entry point for the crisis subcategory and sits at meaningful confidence. The mean is structurally lower than Anxiety or Sleep protocols, consistent with listeners arriving in acute distress states. The distribution concentrates in the 5–8 range with a mode at 8 (13 responses), suggesting a meaningful proportion of listeners do experience significant benefit. pct_pos at 52% is not a failure signal in this context.
H-CR-L1.sphere_plain: Anchor is designed for moments of acute distress, so listeners arrive in some of the most difficult states in the whole collection. Against that backdrop, the results are genuinely encouraging — about half of respondents report meaningful relief, and a notable group report significant benefit. This isn't a protocol that will always produce dramatic shifts, but it appears to be doing what it's designed to do.

H-CR-L2.name: Harbor
H-CR-L2.sphere: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67) is the lowest-mean protocol in the HEALING series, but this is structurally anticipated for a crisis L2 DESCENT. The distribution is relatively flat across the 5–8 range, indicating heterogeneous listener response — likely reflecting wide variation in crisis severity at point of access. The pct_pos of 46% is worth monitoring but does not indicate a protocol problem at current n.
H-CR-L2.sphere_plain: Harbor sees a wider spread of responses than most protocols, which makes sense — people in crisis vary enormously in how they're affected by any given intervention. About half of listeners report benefit. There's no red flag here, just a reminder that this protocol is working with very heterogeneous starting conditions.

H-CR-L3.name: Horizon
H-CR-L3.sphere: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85) shows a slight mean recovery relative to L2 (6.14 → 6.31) with a distribution that spreads across the 4–8 range fairly evenly. The two responses at 10 and absence of responses at 9 produce a slight distributional oddity worth watching as n grows. Meaningful confidence; no actionable pattern at this stage.
H-CR-L3.sphere_plain: Horizon performs comparably to the other crisis protocols, with responses spread fairly evenly across the middle-to-upper range. A small number of people found it exceptionally helpful, and the overall picture is consistent with what's expected for this kind of protocol. No surprises, and no concerns at this stage.

H-SL-L1.name: Lantern
H-SL-L1.sphere: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89) sits at strong confidence with a notably flat distribution across the 6–9 range (11 responses at each of 6, 7, 8, and 9), which is unusual. This plateau shape, rather than a clear mode, could reflect genuine broad utility across a heterogeneous sleep-difficulty population, or it could be an early indicator of two overlapping response populations. Flagging for continued monitoring.
H-SL-L1.sphere_plain: Lantern has a genuinely unusual shape for a sleep protocol — instead of most people clustering around one score, responses are spread very evenly across the 6–9 range. That could mean it's reliably useful for a wide variety of people, or it could mean it's landing very differently depending on who's listening. Either way, average results are strong. Something worth understanding better as more people respond.

H-SL-L2.name: Hammock
H-SL-L2.sphere: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46) is one of the lower-variance protocols in the HEALING series and reaches meaningful confidence. The distribution tightens considerably relative to L1, with a clear mode at 7 (15 responses) and modest spread. The reduced std may reflect listener self-selection or genuine format fit; either way, this is a stable signal.
H-SL-L2.sphere_plain: Hammock is one of the more consistent protocols in the collection — responses cluster neatly around the 7 range with less variation than most. Listeners who reach this level seem to be getting a reliably helpful experience. A stable and encouraging signal.

H-SL-L3.name: Midnight
H-SL-L3.sphere: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57) maintains the mean established at L2 with slightly higher std, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is symmetric around 6–8 with a balanced shape. The Sleep arc (6.97 → 7.04 → 7.03) is remarkably stable across levels, suggesting consistent utility regardless of depth — a notable finding that may inform future routing logic.
H-SL-L3.sphere_plain: Midnight holds almost exactly the same average as Hammock, which is a good sign — the experience doesn't seem to lose effectiveness at the deeper level. The Sleep series as a whole is notably steady across all three levels, which suggests this is a well-calibrated arc.

T-FC-L1.name: Compass
T-FC-L1.sphere: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.4, std=1.77) sits at meaningful confidence with a bimodal-adjacent distribution: a cluster at 4–5 (11 responses combined) and a cluster at 7–8 (18 responses combined). This double-cluster pattern is the clearest bimodal signal in the THRIVING series and warrants a population-split investigation — it may reflect two distinct listener types with different baseline focus capacity arriving at this protocol.
T-FC-L1.sphere_plain: Compass shows an interesting pattern — responses seem to split into two groups, with some people finding it quite helpful and another group finding it less so. That kind of split often suggests the protocol is well-suited to some listeners but not others, rather than being inconsistent overall. Understanding who benefits most from this entry point seems like a useful next question.

T-FC-L2.name: Waypoint
T-FC-L2.sphere: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is clean and right-skewed, with most mass in the 7–9 range. The non-monotonic arc (6.4 → 7.09 → 6.23 at L3) positions this as the performance peak of the FC series — a flag worth understanding, as L2 outperforming L3 is architecturally unexpected.
T-FC-L2.sphere_plain: Waypoint is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity group, with most listeners reporting good outcomes and a clean, positive pattern. It's notable that this middle level outperforms the deepest level — which is worth exploring further. For now, Waypoint looks like a reliable destination for this listener group.

T-FC-L3.name: Summit
T-FC-L3.sphere: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38) is the lowest-performing protocol in THRIVING by mean and pct_pos (45%), sitting at meaningful confidence. The distribution concentrates in the 6–7 range with a thin right tail, indicating limited high-response representation. The performance inversion relative to L2 (7.09 → 6.23) is the most structurally notable anomaly in the THRIVING series and warrants active investigation.
T-FC-L3.sphere_plain: Summit is the one protocol in the Thriving series that shows a clearly below-average picture, with fewer than half of respondents reporting positive benefit. It's not a dramatic underperformance, but it stands out compared to Waypoint below it. The format at this depth may need revisiting — either the structure or who gets routed here.

T-CF-L1.name: Ember
T-CF-L1.sphere: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63) sits at meaningful confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with a mode at 7 (10 responses) and modest spread. The pct_pos of 53% is the lower end for THRIVING but reflects the broader listener pool at L1. No anomalous distribution features detected.
T-CF-L1.sphere_plain: Ember is a solid entry point for Creative Flow, with most listeners reporting moderate to good benefit. Results are a little more variable than in the Sleep series, which may reflect the wider range of people who engage with creative-focus protocols and what they're hoping for. A reasonable starting signal.

T-CF-L2.name: Current
T-CF-L2.sphere: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28) has the tightest std in the Creative Flow subcategory, indicating a notably homogeneous response profile at meaningful confidence. The distribution clusters in the 6–8 range. The slight mean decrease from L1 (6.66 → 6.58) is not structurally significant at these n values, but should be monitored as T-CF-L3 is currently the strongest performer in the arc.
T-CF-L2.sphere_plain: Current shows the most consistent responses in the Creative Flow group — people tend to land in a fairly predictable range, mostly positive. It's a narrower, more settled picture than L1, which may mean listeners at this level have a clearer sense of what they're looking for and are finding it.

T-CF-L3.name: Aurora
T-CF-L3.sphere: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15) is at developing confidence but shows the tightest distribution in the Creative Flow subcategory and the highest mean. The distribution [0,0,0,0,0,1,5,4,6,2,0] is clean and compact. Low std at L3 of a HOLD protocol is a positive signal — it suggests the format is producing consistent outcomes for the self-selected population reaching this level.
T-CF-L3.sphere_plain: Aurora is the most intriguing protocol in Creative Flow. Despite having fewer responses, the picture is unusually clean — most people score it in the 7–8 range with very little variation. If this holds up as more people listen, it could be one of the most reliably effective protocols in the collection. Worth prioritising for more data.

T-MB-L1.name: Stone
T-MB-L1.sphere: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31) is the highest mean in the THRIVING series alongside Aurora, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with a mode at 8 (9 responses) and minimal left-tail mass. The tight std and high pct_pos (69%) make this one of the most consistent-performing protocols in the dataset relative to its n.
T-MB-L1.sphere_plain: Stone is one of the most consistent and well-performing protocols in the entire collection. Results are reliably positive, variation is low, and most listeners seem to find genuine benefit. For a maintenance and balance entry point, this is a strong foundation.

T-MB-L2.name: Axis
T-MB-L2.sphere: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97) shows a marked std increase relative to L1 (1.31 → 1.97), at meaningful confidence. The distribution is spread across the 3–10 range with no clear mode and a notably flat shape. This variance increase at L2 may indicate the protocol attracts a wider ability range at the intermediate level, or that the L1-to-L2 transition introduces friction for some listener types. Worth monitoring.
T-MB-L2.sphere_plain: Axis shows a much wider spread of responses than Stone, which is unexpected. Some listeners respond very well, others considerably less so — and there's no strong central pattern. This level of variation at L2 is worth understanding better. It may be that listeners arriving here have a broader range of needs or starting states than those at L1.

T-MB-L3.name: Orbit
T-MB-L3.sphere: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64) is at developing confidence. The distribution [0,0,0,0,2,0,2,9,0,0,2] is structurally unusual — a strong cluster at 7 (9 responses), isolated responses at 4 and 10, and gaps between. The shape does not yet constitute a reliable pattern at n=15 but the bimodal-adjacent structure (cluster + outliers) should be revisited at n=30+.
T-MB-L3.sphere_plain: Orbit has an unusual shape — the vast majority of responses cluster tightly around 7, with a couple of outliers at the low and high ends. It's too early to draw conclusions with only 15 responses, but the pattern is distinctive enough to watch. If the cluster holds, this could become a very consistent protocol; if those outliers grow, it might signal a listener split.

X-ME-L1.name: Breeze
X-ME-L1.sphere: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71) is at meaningful confidence. The distribution has a mode at 6 (9 responses) with a flat right tail and a secondary cluster at 4–5 (8 responses combined) — a mild bimodal-adjacent shape. pct_pos of 43% is among the lowest in the dataset, consistent with ASCENT protocol dynamics for low-energy listeners. The secondary low cluster warrants monitoring for a potential population mismatch.
X-ME-L1.sphere_plain: Breeze shows a mild split in responses — most listeners are in the positive range, but a meaningful cluster scores it lower. For an entry-level protocol aimed at lifting mood and energy from low states, this level of variation is understandable. The lower-scoring group may be arriving in states that need something different first.

X-ME-L2.name: Sunrise
X-ME-L2.sphere: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64) is at meaningful confidence with a notable mean improvement over L1 (6.29 → 6.48) and higher pct_pos (60% vs 43%). The distribution concentrates in the 7–8 range with modest spread. This is the strongest signal in the Mood & Energy arc and may reflect self-selection of more responsive listeners at L2.
X-ME-L2.sphere_plain: Sunrise is the strongest performer in the Mood & Energy group, with a notably higher proportion of positive responses than either the level below or above it. The picture is more focused and encouraging at this level — possibly because listeners who reach L2 are in a slightly more receptive state. A positive signal worth building on.

X-ME-L3.name: Updraft
X-ME-L3.sphere: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74) is at developing confidence. The mean regresses slightly from L2 (6.48 → 6.28), though the n delta (25 → 18) makes this comparison unreliable. The distribution is spread across the 4–8 range with a mode at 7 (5 responses). The non-monotonic arc pattern across ME (6.29 → 6.48 → 6.28) mirrors the FC arc anomaly and may indicate that L3 ASCENT protocols are encountering a ceiling effect or listener-state mismatch at maximum depth.
X-ME-L3.sphere_plain: Updraft shows a slight dip back from Sunrise's stronger performance, with responses spread across the middle range. It's too early to say whether this reflects something about the protocol itself or simply the small number of listeners who've reached this depth. The pattern mirrors what's seen in the Focus series at L3, which makes it an interesting parallel to investigate.

X-MD-L1.name: Footpath
X-MD-L1.sphere: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97) is the entry ASCENT protocol for Motivation & Drive, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is relatively flat across the 4–9 range, with no clear mode, consistent with high listener variability in baseline motivational state. pct_pos of 43% is the second-lowest in TRANSFORMING and warrants monitoring — though ASCENT protocols targeting low-drive states structurally attract listeners with limited initial responsiveness.
X-MD-L1.sphere_plain: Footpath, the entry point for Motivation & Drive, is reaching listeners in some of the most difficult starting states in the collection. Results are mixed, with responses spread across a wide range and fewer than half reporting clear benefit. This is a structurally hard ask — supporting low-motivation states — and the picture isn't alarming, but it does warrant attention as data grows.

X-MD-L2.name: Trail
X-MD-L2.sphere: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, std=1.57) is at developing confidence with the lowest pct_pos in the dataset (36%) and the lowest mean in the TRANSFORMING series. The distribution clusters in the 5–7 range with a thin right tail. Even accounting for small n and ASCENT context, this is the weakest aggregate signal in the dataset and should be treated as a development priority as n grows.
X-MD-L2.sphere_plain: Trail is the one protocol in the collection that stands out as potentially needing a closer look. The proportion of listeners reporting benefit is lower than any other protocol, and average scores are the lowest in the Transforming series. It's still early, and the context — listeners in low-motivation states attempting a more demanding protocol — explains some of this. But it's worth asking whether the path from L1 to L2 is working as intended.

X-MD-L3.name: Switchback
X-MD-L3.sphere: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31) is at developing confidence with the second-highest std in the dataset. The distribution [0,0,0,1,2,0,2,1,2,3,1] is spread and irregular, with a right-leaning concentration despite the low-end mass. The mean recovery from L2 (6.0 → 6.92) is notable but not reliable at n=12. The high std is consistent with extreme baseline variability in this listener population.
X-MD-L3.sphere_plain: Switchback has the widest spread of responses in the whole collection, with scores ranging from very low to quite high. The average is actually the strongest in the Motivation & Drive arc, but that average hides a lot of variation. With only 12 responses, this is a very early picture — and the variation likely reflects how different people's motivational states are when they reach this level.

X-MC-L1.name: Cavern
X-MC-L1.sphere: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56) is the entry point for the TRANSFORMING Mind & Clarity subcategory, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is unimodal around 6–8 with modest spread and a thin low tail. pct_pos at 50% is expected for a DESCENT protocol in TRANSFORMING. No anomalous structure detected.
X-MC-L1.sphere_plain: Cavern is a reasonable starting point for the Mind & Clarity protocols in the Transforming series, with responses mainly in the positive range and no obvious problems. About half of listeners report meaningful benefit — which is in line with what's expected for this type of protocol aimed at people in lower-affect states.

X-MC-L2.name: Starlight
X-MC-L2.sphere: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37) is at developing confidence with a compact distribution clustering in the 6–7 range. The tight std relative to L1 may reflect listener self-selection. The flat mean across L1 and L2 (6.62 → 6.59) suggests no arc effect yet detectable — expected given n constraints.
X-MC-L2.sphere_plain: Starlight is building a consistent picture at this early stage, with responses clustering in the middle-positive range and less variation than L1. There's not enough data yet to say anything definitive, but the early shape is stable and not concerning.

X-MC-L3.name: Cosmos
X-MC-L3.sphere: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32) has the highest std and lowest mean in the Mind & Clarity arc, at developing confidence. The distribution [0,0,1,1,2,1,0,3,1,1,0] is thin and irregular, with a gap at 6 and spread across the full range. At n=10, no pattern conclusions are warranted; however, the std=2.32 is an early flag that this population may be highly heterogeneous or that the protocol is encountering mismatched listener states. Prioritise n growth before any development decisions.
X-MC-L3.sphere_plain: Cosmos has very few responses so far, and the spread of those responses is wide — ranging from quite low to quite high. It would be premature to draw any conclusions here; this protocol simply needs more listeners before the picture becomes meaningful. The spread could reflect many things and shouldn't be treated as a problem signal yet.

sphere_commentary.overview: Across 1,000 responses spanning 30 protocols, the aggregate signal is cautiously positive: series means cluster between 6.36 and 6.78, with positive-response rates generally in the 50–70% range. Standard deviations hovering near 1.5–1.9 throughout suggest meaningful population diversity rather than protocol inconsistency — a structurally expected finding at this stage of data accumulation. No series is underperforming in a way that warrants concern; the gradient from HEALING to TRANSFORMING is consistent with the ascending-difficulty arc built into the framework.
sphere_commentary.cross_series: HEALING leads on both mean (6.78) and sample depth (n=582), which is expected given its entry-level positioning and likely broader listener base — the Sleep and Anxiety subcategories in particular are performing with notable consistency. THRIVING sits close behind (mean=6.73, n=242) with notably tighter standard deviations, suggesting a more homogeneous responder population, possibly listeners who self-select into optimisation-oriented content with clearer baseline stability. TRANSFORMING shows the lowest mean (6.36, n=176) and highest variance across its subcategories, consistent with the hypothesis that ASCENT protocols targeting low-affect states attract listeners in more variable starting conditions — this is a structural signal, not a performance failure.
sphere_commentary.anomalies: Three patterns warrant flagging. First, X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32) shows the highest standard deviation in the dataset combined with the lowest mean in the TRANSFORMING series — the distribution [0,0,1,1,2,1,0,3,1,1,0] has a notable gap at score 6, which could indicate early bimodal pressure; however n=10 means this is noise-level data and no conclusion is justified yet. Second, T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15) shows a strikingly compressed distribution [0,0,0,0,2,0,2,9,0,0,2] with a cluster at 7 and isolated responses at 4 and 10 — the shape is unusual and warrants watching as n grows; the gap between the cluster and the high outlier could reflect a subgroup responding very differently. Third, X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, pct_pos=36%) has the lowest positive-response rate in the dataset, which stands out even accounting for the ASCENT protocol context and small n — this is worth monitoring as a potential routing or sequencing issue rather than a content failure.
sphere_commentary.development_signals: 1. X-MD-L2 | Trail | TRANSFORMING | Motivation & Drive | ASCENT | n=14: Lowest pct_pos in the dataset at 36%, with a mean of 6.0 and distribution showing clustering in the 5–6 range. Even granting small-n caution, this is the weakest signal in Motivation & Drive. Suggested action: audit the Adviser routing logic feeding into Trail — listeners arriving here may not have sufficient arousal baseline for an ASCENT protocol at L2 difficulty. Consider whether a bridging micro-protocol or a revised L1-to-L2 threshold is warranted before expanding this protocol's reach. 2. T-FC-L3 | Summit | THRIVING | Focus & Clarity | HOLD | n=22, mean=6.23, pct_pos=45%: This is the lowest-performing protocol in THRIVING and underperforms relative to T-FC-L1 (mean=6.4) and especially T-FC-L2 (mean=7.09). The non-monotonic performance across the L1–L2–L3 arc — with L2 outperforming L3 — is a development-relevant anomaly. Suggested action: investigate whether Summit's phi-sigmoid curve architecture is appropriate for its target population; L3 HOLD protocols may require longer hold phases to produce entrainment in high-functioning listeners who are harder to shift. A hold-phase extension experiment is warranted. 3. H-CR series (Anchor, Harbor, Horizon) | HEALING | Acute Distress / Crisis | DESCENT | combined n=112: Positive-response rates sit at 52%, 46%, and 48% respectively — structurally expected given crisis-state entry conditions, but the distribution shapes across all three show a notable concentration in the 5–7 band with thin tails above 8. This suggests the protocols are stabilising listeners but not producing strong uplift. Suggested action: consider developing a post-crisis HOLD variant (e.g. H-CR-HOLD-L1) designed to extend the window after initial stabilisation — the current arc may be terminating before the listener can consolidate the shift. 4. T-CF-L3 | Aurora | THRIVING | Creative Flow | HOLD | n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15: This is one of the tightest distributions in the dataset and the highest mean in THRIVING, but sits at developing confidence with n=18. The distribution [0,0,0,0,0,1,5,4,6,2,0] is notably clean. Suggested action: prioritise listener volume routing to Aurora to build n toward the 50-response meaningful-confidence threshold — this protocol shows early signs of being a reliable performer and the signal quality justifies faster validation investment.

by_protocol.H-AX-L1: H-AX-L1 (Meadow, n=65, mean=7.14, std=1.89) is the highest-mean protocol in the HEALING series and sits at strong confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with modal response at 6 and 8-9, and a thin low tail, suggesting broad positive response with moderate spread. The std of 1.89 is among the higher values in the Anxiety subcategory and warrants monitoring for a latent bimodal structure as n grows, though current shape does not confirm one.
by_protocol.H-AX-L2: H-AX-L2 (Cottage, n=51, mean=6.9, std=1.63) reaches strong confidence with a tighter distribution than L1. The modal cluster sits at 7 (16 responses) with a clean right-skew and minimal low-end mass. The reduced std relative to L1 may reflect self-selection: listeners who progress to L2 may have greater prior familiarity with the format, producing more homogeneous outcomes.
by_protocol.H-AX-L3: H-AX-L3 (Valley, n=38, mean=6.97, std=1.81) sits at meaningful confidence with a distribution that broadly mirrors L1 in shape, including a notable tail above 8. The consistency of mean scores across the AX arc (7.14 → 6.90 → 6.97) is a positive signal; there is no evidence of quality degradation at deeper levels. The std remains moderate; subgroup investigation not yet indicated.
by_protocol.H-CR-L1: H-CR-L1 (Anchor, n=48, mean=6.35, std=1.71) is the entry point for the crisis subcategory and sits at meaningful confidence. The mean is structurally lower than Anxiety or Sleep protocols, consistent with listeners arriving in acute distress states. The distribution concentrates in the 5–8 range with a mode at 8 (13 responses), suggesting a meaningful proportion of listeners do experience significant benefit. pct_pos at 52% is not a failure signal in this context.
by_protocol.H-CR-L2: H-CR-L2 (Harbor, n=35, mean=6.14, std=1.67) is the lowest-mean protocol in the HEALING series, but this is structurally anticipated for a crisis L2 DESCENT. The distribution is relatively flat across the 5–8 range, indicating heterogeneous listener response — likely reflecting wide variation in crisis severity at point of access. The pct_pos of 46% is worth monitoring but does not indicate a protocol problem at current n.
by_protocol.H-CR-L3: H-CR-L3 (Horizon, n=29, mean=6.31, std=1.85) shows a slight mean recovery relative to L2 (6.14 → 6.31) with a distribution that spreads across the 4–8 range fairly evenly. The two responses at 10 and absence of responses at 9 produce a slight distributional oddity worth watching as n grows. Meaningful confidence; no actionable pattern at this stage.
by_protocol.H-OS-L1: H-OS-L1 (Pebble, n=73, mean=6.55, std=1.80) is the highest-n protocol in the HEALING series and sits at strong confidence. The distribution is broadly unimodal with a mode at 6 (19 responses), a right shoulder extending to 9, and minor left-tail mass at 3–4. The mean is modestly below the Anxiety subcategory, consistent with the different regulatory target. No bimodal structure detected; the spread reflects expected population diversity.
by_protocol.H-OS-L2: H-OS-L2 (Rainfall, n=58, mean=6.71, std=1.85) shows a modest improvement over L1 in mean with comparable std, suggesting the protocol arc is functioning as designed. The distribution has a dual peak at 7 and 8 (15 responses combined) with a secondary mass at 4. The std remains consistent with population diversity rather than bimodality; the left-tail mass at 4 (7 responses) may reflect a subpopulation with limited responsiveness to longer DESCENT formats.
by_protocol.H-OS-L3: H-OS-L3 (Glacier, n=44, mean=6.98, std=1.78) reaches meaningful confidence with the highest mean in the Over-stimulation subcategory. The distribution is relatively symmetric around 7–8, with tails present but not heavy. The ascending mean across the OS arc (6.55 → 6.71 → 6.98) is a clean and encouraging signal, consistent with the phi-sigmoid architecture producing stronger entrainment at longer durations for this listener group.
by_protocol.H-SL-L1: H-SL-L1 (Lantern, n=61, mean=6.97, std=1.89) sits at strong confidence with a notably flat distribution across the 6–9 range (11 responses at each of 6, 7, 8, and 9), which is unusual. This plateau shape, rather than a clear mode, could reflect genuine broad utility across a heterogeneous sleep-difficulty population, or it could be an early indicator of two overlapping response populations. Flagging for continued monitoring.
by_protocol.H-SL-L2: H-SL-L2 (Hammock, n=47, mean=7.04, std=1.46) is one of the lower-variance protocols in the HEALING series and reaches meaningful confidence. The distribution tightens considerably relative to L1, with a clear mode at 7 (15 responses) and modest spread. The reduced std may reflect listener self-selection or genuine format fit; either way, this is a stable signal.
by_protocol.H-SL-L3: H-SL-L3 (Midnight, n=33, mean=7.03, std=1.57) maintains the mean established at L2 with slightly higher std, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is symmetric around 6–8 with a balanced shape. The Sleep arc (6.97 → 7.04 → 7.03) is remarkably stable across levels, suggesting consistent utility regardless of depth — a notable finding that may inform future routing logic.
by_protocol.T-CF-L1: T-CF-L1 (Ember, n=38, mean=6.66, std=1.63) sits at meaningful confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with a mode at 7 (10 responses) and modest spread. The pct_pos of 53% is the lower end for THRIVING but reflects the broader listener pool at L1. No anomalous distribution features detected.
by_protocol.T-CF-L2: T-CF-L2 (Current, n=24, mean=6.58, std=1.28) has the tightest std in the Creative Flow subcategory, indicating a notably homogeneous response profile at meaningful confidence. The distribution clusters in the 6–8 range. The slight mean decrease from L1 (6.66 → 6.58) is not structurally significant at these n values, but should be monitored as T-CF-L3 is currently the strongest performer in the arc.
by_protocol.T-CF-L3: T-CF-L3 (Aurora, n=18, mean=7.17, std=1.15) is at developing confidence but shows the tightest distribution in the Creative Flow subcategory and the highest mean. The distribution [0,0,0,0,0,1,5,4,6,2,0] is clean and compact. Low std at L3 of a HOLD protocol is a positive signal — it suggests the format is producing consistent outcomes for the self-selected population reaching this level.
by_protocol.T-FC-L1: T-FC-L1 (Compass, n=42, mean=6.4, std=1.77) sits at meaningful confidence with a bimodal-adjacent distribution: a cluster at 4–5 (11 responses combined) and a cluster at 7–8 (18 responses combined). This double-cluster pattern is the clearest bimodal signal in the THRIVING series and warrants a population-split investigation — it may reflect two distinct listener types with different baseline focus capacity arriving at this protocol.
by_protocol.T-FC-L2: T-FC-L2 (Waypoint, n=32, mean=7.09, std=1.51) is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity subcategory, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is clean and right-skewed, with most mass in the 7–9 range. The non-monotonic arc (6.4 → 7.09 → 6.23 at L3) positions this as the performance peak of the FC series — a flag worth understanding, as L2 outperforming L3 is architecturally unexpected.
by_protocol.T-FC-L3: T-FC-L3 (Summit, n=22, mean=6.23, std=1.38) is the lowest-performing protocol in THRIVING by mean and pct_pos (45%), sitting at meaningful confidence. The distribution concentrates in the 6–7 range with a thin right tail, indicating limited high-response representation. The performance inversion relative to L2 (7.09 → 6.23) is the most structurally notable anomaly in the THRIVING series and warrants active investigation.
by_protocol.T-MB-L1: T-MB-L1 (Stone, n=29, mean=7.17, std=1.31) is the highest mean in the THRIVING series alongside Aurora, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is right-skewed with a mode at 8 (9 responses) and minimal left-tail mass. The tight std and high pct_pos (69%) make this one of the most consistent-performing protocols in the dataset relative to its n.
by_protocol.T-MB-L2: T-MB-L2 (Axis, n=22, mean=6.59, std=1.97) shows a marked std increase relative to L1 (1.31 → 1.97), at meaningful confidence. The distribution is spread across the 3–10 range with no clear mode and a notably flat shape. This variance increase at L2 may indicate the protocol attracts a wider ability range at the intermediate level, or that the L1-to-L2 transition introduces friction for some listener types. Worth monitoring.
by_protocol.T-MB-L3: T-MB-L3 (Orbit, n=15, mean=6.87, std=1.64) is at developing confidence. The distribution [0,0,0,0,2,0,2,9,0,0,2] is structurally unusual — a strong cluster at 7 (9 responses), isolated responses at 4 and 10, and gaps between. The shape does not yet constitute a reliable pattern at n=15 but the bimodal-adjacent structure (cluster + outliers) should be revisited at n=30+.
by_protocol.X-MC-L1: X-MC-L1 (Cavern, n=24, mean=6.62, std=1.56) is the entry point for the TRANSFORMING Mind & Clarity subcategory, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is unimodal around 6–8 with modest spread and a thin low tail. pct_pos at 50% is expected for a DESCENT protocol in TRANSFORMING. No anomalous structure detected.
by_protocol.X-MC-L2: X-MC-L2 (Starlight, n=17, mean=6.59, std=1.37) is at developing confidence with a compact distribution clustering in the 6–7 range. The tight std relative to L1 may reflect listener self-selection. The flat mean across L1 and L2 (6.62 → 6.59) suggests no arc effect yet detectable — expected given n constraints.
by_protocol.X-MC-L3: X-MC-L3 (Cosmos, n=10, mean=5.6, std=2.32) has the highest std and lowest mean in the Mind & Clarity arc, at developing confidence. The distribution [0,0,1,1,2,1,0,3,1,1,0] is thin and irregular, with a gap at 6 and spread across the full range. At n=10, no pattern conclusions are warranted; however, the std=2.32 is an early flag that this population may be highly heterogeneous or that the protocol is encountering mismatched listener states. Prioritise n growth before any development decisions.
by_protocol.X-MD-L1: X-MD-L1 (Footpath, n=21, mean=6.24, std=1.97) is the entry ASCENT protocol for Motivation & Drive, at meaningful confidence. The distribution is relatively flat across the 4–9 range, with no clear mode, consistent with high listener variability in baseline motivational state. pct_pos of 43% is the second-lowest in TRANSFORMING and warrants monitoring — though ASCENT protocols targeting low-drive states structurally attract listeners with limited initial responsiveness.
by_protocol.X-MD-L2: X-MD-L2 (Trail, n=14, mean=6.0, std=1.57) is at developing confidence with the lowest pct_pos in the dataset (36%) and the lowest mean in the TRANSFORMING series. The distribution clusters in the 5–7 range with a thin right tail. Even accounting for small n and ASCENT context, this is the weakest aggregate signal in the dataset and should be treated as a development priority as n grows.
by_protocol.X-MD-L3: X-MD-L3 (Switchback, n=12, mean=6.92, std=2.31) is at developing confidence with the second-highest std in the dataset. The distribution [0,0,0,1,2,0,2,1,2,3,1] is spread and irregular, with a right-leaning concentration despite the low-end mass. The mean recovery from L2 (6.0 → 6.92) is notable but not reliable at n=12. The high std is consistent with extreme baseline variability in this listener population.
by_protocol.X-ME-L1: X-ME-L1 (Breeze, n=35, mean=6.29, std=1.71) is at meaningful confidence. The distribution has a mode at 6 (9 responses) with a flat right tail and a secondary cluster at 4–5 (8 responses combined) — a mild bimodal-adjacent shape. pct_pos of 43% is among the lowest in the dataset, consistent with ASCENT protocol dynamics for low-energy listeners. The secondary low cluster warrants monitoring for a potential population mismatch.
by_protocol.X-ME-L2: X-ME-L2 (Sunrise, n=25, mean=6.48, std=1.64) is at meaningful confidence with a notable mean improvement over L1 (6.29 → 6.48) and higher pct_pos (60% vs 43%). The distribution concentrates in the 7–8 range with modest spread. This is the strongest signal in the Mood & Energy arc and may reflect self-selection of more responsive listeners at L2.
by_protocol.X-ME-L3: X-ME-L3 (Updraft, n=18, mean=6.28, std=1.74) is at developing confidence. The mean regresses slightly from L2 (6.48 → 6.28), though the n delta (25 → 18) makes this comparison unreliable. The distribution is spread across the 4–8 range with a mode at 7 (5 responses). The non-monotonic arc pattern across ME (6.29 → 6.48 → 6.28) mirrors the FC arc anomaly and may indicate that L3 ASCENT protocols are encountering a ceiling effect or listener-state mismatch at maximum depth.

by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L1: Meadow is one of the better-performing protocols in the collection, with most listeners reporting meaningful relief from anxiety and stress. Results do vary from person to person — some find it very helpful, a small number less so — but overall the signal is encouraging and based on enough responses to feel reliable.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L2: Cottage shows a tighter, more consistent pattern than Meadow, with most responses clustering around the higher end of the scale. Listeners who arrive at L2 seem to respond a bit more predictably — which likely reflects that they already have some experience with the format. A solid, steady performer.
by_protocol_plain.H-AX-L3: Valley holds up well across a meaningful number of responses, with scores staying consistent with the earlier levels in the Anxiety series. There's no sign of the experience becoming less effective at the deeper level — if anything, it holds its own. A positive result for the arc as a whole.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L1: Anchor is designed for moments of acute distress, so listeners arrive in some of the most difficult states in the whole collection. Against that backdrop, the results are genuinely encouraging — about half of respondents report meaningful relief, and a notable group report significant benefit. This isn't a protocol that will always produce dramatic shifts, but it appears to be doing what it's designed to do.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L2: Harbor sees a wider spread of responses than most protocols, which makes sense — people in crisis vary enormously in how they're affected by any given intervention. About half of listeners report benefit. There's no red flag here, just a reminder that this protocol is working with very heterogeneous starting conditions.
by_protocol_plain.H-CR-L3: Horizon performs comparably to the other crisis protocols, with responses spread fairly evenly across the middle-to-upper range. A small number of people found it exceptionally helpful, and the overall picture is consistent with what's expected for this kind of protocol. No surprises, and no concerns at this stage.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L1: Pebble has the most responses of any protocol in the collection, which gives us good confidence in the picture it paints. Most listeners report meaningful benefit, with results concentrated around the 6–8 range. A small number find it less helpful — likely people whose sensory experience of the music doesn't connect. Broadly reliable as an entry point for over-stimulation.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L2: Rainfall shows a modest improvement over Pebble, with more responses in the higher range and a pattern that suggests the arc is working as intended. A small cluster of listeners appears to respond less well — possibly those who find longer protocols harder to engage with when overstimulated. Worth keeping an eye on as more data comes in.
by_protocol_plain.H-OS-L3: Glacier is the strongest performer in the Over-stimulation group, with the highest average score of the three and a fairly balanced spread. The pattern across all three levels — gently improving as you go deeper — is encouraging. Longer protocols appear to be doing more for this listener group, which aligns with how the music is designed to work.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L1: Lantern has a genuinely unusual shape for a sleep protocol — instead of most people clustering around one score, responses are spread very evenly across the 6–9 range. That could mean it's reliably useful for a wide variety of people, or it could mean it's landing very differently depending on who's listening. Either way, average results are strong. Something worth understanding better as more people respond.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L2: Hammock is one of the more consistent protocols in the collection — responses cluster neatly around the 7 range with less variation than most. Listeners who reach this level seem to be getting a reliably helpful experience. A stable and encouraging signal.
by_protocol_plain.H-SL-L3: Midnight holds almost exactly the same average as Hammock, which is a good sign — the experience doesn't seem to lose effectiveness at the deeper level. The Sleep series as a whole is notably steady across all three levels, which suggests this is a well-calibrated arc.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L1: Ember is a solid entry point for Creative Flow, with most listeners reporting moderate to good benefit. Results are a little more variable than in the Sleep series, which may reflect the wider range of people who engage with creative-focus protocols and what they're hoping for. A reasonable starting signal.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L2: Current shows the most consistent responses in the Creative Flow group — people tend to land in a fairly predictable range, mostly positive. It's a narrower, more settled picture than L1, which may mean listeners at this level have a clearer sense of what they're looking for and are finding it.
by_protocol_plain.T-CF-L3: Aurora is the most intriguing protocol in Creative Flow. Despite having fewer responses, the picture is unusually clean — most people score it in the 7–8 range with very little variation. If this holds up as more people listen, it could be one of the most reliably effective protocols in the collection. Worth prioritising for more data.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L1: Compass shows an interesting pattern — responses seem to split into two groups, with some people finding it quite helpful and another group finding it less so. That kind of split often suggests the protocol is well-suited to some listeners but not others, rather than being inconsistent overall. Understanding who benefits most from this entry point seems like a useful next question.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L2: Waypoint is the strongest performer in the Focus & Clarity group, with most listeners reporting good outcomes and a clean, positive pattern. It's notable that this middle level outperforms the deepest level — which is worth exploring further. For now, Waypoint looks like a reliable destination for this listener group.
by_protocol_plain.T-FC-L3: Summit is the one protocol in the Thriving series that shows a clearly below-average picture, with fewer than half of respondents reporting positive benefit. It's not a dramatic underperformance, but it stands out compared to Waypoint below it. The format at this depth may need revisiting — either the structure or who gets routed here.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L1: Stone is one of the most consistent and well-performing protocols in the entire collection. Results are reliably positive, variation is low, and most listeners seem to find genuine benefit. For a maintenance and balance entry point, this is a strong foundation.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L2: Axis shows a much wider spread of responses than Stone, which is unexpected. Some listeners respond very well, others considerably less so — and there's no strong central pattern. This level of variation at L2 is worth understanding better. It may be that listeners arriving here have a broader range of needs or starting states than those at L1.
by_protocol_plain.T-MB-L3: Orbit has an unusual shape — the vast majority of responses cluster tightly around 7, with a couple of outliers at the low and high ends. It's too early to draw conclusions with only 15 responses, but the pattern is distinctive enough to watch. If the cluster holds, this could become a very consistent protocol; if those outliers grow, it might signal a listener split.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L1: Cavern is a reasonable starting point for the Mind & Clarity protocols in the Transforming series, with responses mainly in the positive range and no obvious problems. About half of listeners report meaningful benefit — which is in line with what's expected for this type of protocol aimed at people in lower-affect states.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L2: Starlight is building a consistent picture at this early stage, with responses clustering in the middle-positive range and less variation than L1. There's not enough data yet to say anything definitive, but the early shape is stable and not concerning.
by_protocol_plain.X-MC-L3: Cosmos has very few responses so far, and the spread of those responses is wide — ranging from quite low to quite high. It would be premature to draw any conclusions here; this protocol simply needs more listeners before the picture becomes meaningful. The spread could reflect many things and shouldn't be treated as a problem signal yet.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L1: Footpath, the entry point for Motivation & Drive, is reaching listeners in some of the most difficult starting states in the collection. Results are mixed, with responses spread across a wide range and fewer than half reporting clear benefit. This is a structurally hard ask — supporting low-motivation states — and the picture isn't alarming, but it does warrant attention as data grows.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L2: Trail is the one protocol in the collection that stands out as potentially needing a closer look. The proportion of listeners reporting benefit is lower than any other protocol, and average scores are the lowest in the Transforming series. It's still early, and the context — listeners in low-motivation states attempting a more demanding protocol — explains some of this. But it's worth asking whether the path from L1 to L2 is working as intended.
by_protocol_plain.X-MD-L3: Switchback has the widest spread of responses in the whole collection, with scores ranging from very low to quite high. The average is actually the strongest in the Motivation & Drive arc, but that average hides a lot of variation. With only 12 responses, this is a very early picture — and the variation likely reflects how different people's motivational states are when they reach this level.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L1: Breeze shows a mild split in responses — most listeners are in the positive range, but a meaningful cluster scores it lower. For an entry-level protocol aimed at lifting mood and energy from low states, this level of variation is understandable. The lower-scoring group may be arriving in states that need something different first.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L2: Sunrise is the strongest performer in the Mood & Energy group, with a notably higher proportion of positive responses than either the level below or above it. The picture is more focused and encouraging at this level — possibly because listeners who reach L2 are in a slightly more receptive state. A positive signal worth building on.
by_protocol_plain.X-ME-L3: Updraft shows a slight dip back from Sunrise's stronger performance, with responses spread across the middle range. It's too early to say whether this reflects something about the protocol itself or simply the small number of listeners who've reached this depth. The pattern mirrors what's seen in the Focus series at L3, which makes it an interesting parallel to investigate.
